prompt: According to Revelation 1, from whom is the revelation given to Jesus Christ?
correct_answer: God
explanation: The book opens by saying it is 'The Revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave him' (Revelation 1:1).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, to whom was the revelation signified through an angel?
correct_answer: John
explanation: God gave the revelation to Jesus, who sent and signified it by his angel to his servant John (Revelation 1:1).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, how many churches are addressed in Asia?
correct_answer: Seven
explanation: John writes 'to the seven assemblies that are in Asia' (Revelation 1:4).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, on what island was John when he received the vision?
correct_answer: Patmos
explanation: John says he was on the island called Patmos (Revelation 1:9).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, on what day was John in the Spirit?
correct_answer: The Lord's day
explanation: John says, 'I was in the Spirit on the Lord's day' (Revelation 1:10).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what did John hear behind him?
choices_json: A loud voice like a trumpet
A choir of angels singing softly
The sound of rushing water from the shore
correct_answer: A loud voice like a trumpet
explanation: John heard behind him 'a loud voice, like a trumpet' (Revelation 1:10).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what did John see when he turned to see the voice?
correct_answer: Seven golden lampstands
explanation: When John turned, he saw seven golden lampstands (Revelation 1:12).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, why is the one who reads and those who hear called blessed?
choices_json: Because they keep the things written in it
Because they understand every mystery immediately
correct_answer: Because they keep the things written in it
explanation: The blessing is for the one who reads and those who hear and keep the things written in the prophecy (Revelation 1:3).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what reason is given for the urgency of the prophecy?
correct_answer: The time is at hand
explanation: John says the time is at hand (Revelation 1:3).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, from whom do grace and peace come in John's greeting?
choices_json: From him who is, who was, and who is to come; from the seven Spirits; and from Jesus Christ
From the apostles in Jerusalem only
correct_answer: From him who is, who was, and who is to come; from the seven Spirits; and from Jesus Christ
explanation: John's greeting names these three sources of grace and peace (Revelation 1:4-5).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what has Jesus made believers to be for his God and Father?
correct_answer: A Kingdom and priests
explanation: Jesus made believers 'to be a Kingdom, priests to his God and Father' (Revelation 1:6).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what will all the tribes of the earth do when he comes with the clouds?
correct_answer: Mourn over him
explanation: John says all the tribes of the earth will mourn over him (Revelation 1:7).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, for what reasons was John on Patmos?
choices_json: For the word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ
For preaching in the synagogues of Asia
For writing the Gospel of Luke
correct_answer: For the word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ
explanation: John says he was on Patmos for the word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ (Revelation 1:9).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what was John told to do with what he saw?
choices_json: Write it in a book and send it to the seven churches
Tell it only to Peter and Paul
Seal it until the distant future
Teach it in the temple courts
correct_answer: Write it in a book and send it to the seven churches
explanation: The voice told John to write what he saw in a book and send it to the seven churches (Revelation 1:11).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, where was the one like a son of man standing?
choices_json: In the middle of the seven lampstands
At the gate of the city
correct_answer: In the middle of the seven lampstands
explanation: John saw one like a son of man in the middle of the seven lampstands (Revelation 1:13).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what does the repeated emphasis on testimony show about John's role?
choices_json: He serves as a faithful witness to what he saw and heard from God
He is writing fiction to encourage courage
He is guessing about future events
correct_answer: He serves as a faithful witness to what he saw and heard from God
explanation: John bears witness to the word of God, the testimony of Jesus Christ, and all that he saw (Revelation 1:2, 9).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what is suggested by calling Jesus 'the faithful witness, the firstborn from the dead, and the ruler of the kings of the earth'?
choices_json: He is trustworthy, victorious over death, and sovereign over earthly power
He is only an example of patient suffering
He rules one church but not the nations
He came only to judge Israel
correct_answer: He is trustworthy, victorious over death, and sovereign over earthly power
explanation: These titles present Jesus as truthful, risen, and supreme over earthly rulers (Revelation 1:5).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what does John's falling at Jesus' feet like a dead man reveal?
choices_json: The overwhelming glory and authority of the risen Christ
A sign that the vision was ending
correct_answer: The overwhelming glory and authority of the risen Christ
explanation: John's reaction shows the majesty of the one he sees, and Jesus comforts him afterward (Revelation 1:17).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what is implied by Jesus holding the keys of Death and of Hades?
choices_json: He has authority over death and the realm of the dead
He needs help from angels to judge
He rules only the dead and not the living
He is locked away from the churches
correct_answer: He has authority over death and the realm of the dead
explanation: Jesus says he has the keys of Death and of Hades, indicating full authority over them (Revelation 1:18).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, why does the image of Jesus among the lampstands matter for the churches?
choices_json: It shows he is present among and attentive to the churches
It means the churches have become angels
It proves the churches are in heaven already
It shows John is replacing Jesus as leader
correct_answer: It shows he is present among and attentive to the churches
explanation: Jesus stands in the midst of the lampstands, and the lampstands are the seven churches (Revelation 1:13, 20).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what is the force of saying Jesus 'loves us and freed us from our sins by his blood'?
choices_json: His sacrificial death is the basis of redemption and covenant identity
His example alone inspires moral reform
His blood is only symbolic of suffering
His love applies only to John
correct_answer: His sacrificial death is the basis of redemption and covenant identity
explanation: The text joins Jesus' love, release from sins, and the making of a kingdom and priests (Revelation 1:5-6).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what is communicated by the title 'the Alpha and the Omega'?
choices_json: God's completeness and sovereignty from beginning to end
The end of prophecy for the churches
A secret code for the churches of Asia
John's authorship of the Old Testament
correct_answer: God's completeness and sovereignty from beginning to end
explanation: The title Alpha and Omega expresses divine fullness and lordship over all history (Revelation 1:8).

prompt: How does Revelation 1 connect the suffering of John with the reign of Jesus?
choices_json: John shares in tribulation and perseverance while Jesus is shown as the living ruler over death and kings
John's suffering proves Jesus has stopped reigning
Jesus reigns only after the churches are removed
John suffers because he failed to obey the vision
correct_answer: John shares in tribulation and perseverance while Jesus is shown as the living ruler over death and kings
explanation: John is a brother and partner in tribulation and perseverance, while Jesus is presented as ruler of kings and the living one (Revelation 1:5, 9, 18).

prompt: How does Revelation 1 prepare the reader for the messages to the churches that follow?
choices_json: It presents Christ as glorious, present among the churches, and fully aware of them
It mainly explains the geography of Asia
It focuses only on the future new Jerusalem
It removes the need for correction and warning
correct_answer: It presents Christ as glorious, present among the churches, and fully aware of them
explanation: The vision of Christ among the lampstands sets up his direct address to the churches in chapters 2-3 (Revelation 1:12-20).

prompt: How does Revelation 1 balance comfort and awe in its portrayal of Jesus?
choices_json: Jesus terrifies John with glory, then reassures him with 'Don't be afraid'
Jesus hides his power so John stays calm
Jesus speaks only judgment without mercy
Jesus leaves John unconscious and silent
correct_answer: Jesus terrifies John with glory, then reassures him with 'Don't be afraid'
explanation: John falls as though dead, but Jesus lays his right hand on him and says not to fear (Revelation 1:17).

prompt: How does Revelation 1 frame the whole book in relation to time?
choices_json: The revelation concerns things that must soon happen, yet it is spoken by the eternal Lord
The book is only about the distant past
The vision is postponed until another age
correct_answer: The revelation concerns things that must soon happen, yet it is spoken by the eternal Lord
explanation: The book opens with things that must soon happen and the time being at hand, while God is described as eternal (Revelation 1:1, 3, 8).

prompt: What role does the interpretation of the stars and lampstands play in Revelation 1?
choices_json: It shows that the vision's symbols are meaningful and tied to real churches and their angels
It proves every detail in the book must remain unexplained
It cancels the literal reality of the churches
It limits the book to private mystical experience
correct_answer: It shows that the vision's symbols are meaningful and tied to real churches and their angels
explanation: Jesus explains that the seven stars are the angels of the churches and the lampstands are the churches (Revelation 1:20).

prompt: Why is the description of Jesus in Revelation 1 important before any church is evaluated?
choices_json: The churches are judged and comforted by the risen Christ's authority, not by human opinion
It shows John will be the final judge of the churches
It means the churches no longer matter
It replaces obedience with visionary experience
correct_answer: The churches are judged and comforted by the risen Christ's authority, not by human opinion
explanation: Christ's majestic identity grounds the later exhortations, rebukes, and promises to the churches (Revelation 1:13-20).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what does the combination of priestly and royal language applied to believers suggest?
choices_json: Jesus' redemptive work creates a people who belong to God and serve him in consecrated dignity
Believers replace all earthly governments immediately
Only church leaders become priests to God
The churches are freed from all suffering
correct_answer: Jesus' redemptive work creates a people who belong to God and serve him in consecrated dignity
explanation: Jesus freed believers from sins and made them a kingdom and priests to his God and Father (Revelation 1:5-6).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, how do Jesus' death and present life shape the meaning of the vision?
choices_json: The one John sees is the crucified and risen Lord who now reigns forever
The vision concerns only a future messiah not yet revealed
Jesus was once alive spiritually but not bodily
The resurrection is not important to the churches
correct_answer: The one John sees is the crucified and risen Lord who now reigns forever
explanation: Jesus says, 'I was dead, and behold, I am alive forever and ever' (Revelation 1:18).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, why is Jesus' coming with the clouds presented as universal in impact?
choices_json: Every eye will see him, including those who pierced him, and all tribes of the earth will mourn
Only the churches of Asia will know he came
His coming is invisible to the nations
It affects heaven but not earth
correct_answer: Every eye will see him, including those who pierced him, and all tribes of the earth will mourn
explanation: John emphasizes the universality of Christ's appearing and its effect on all peoples (Revelation 1:7).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what theological weight is carried by the statement that Jesus holds the seven stars in his right hand?
choices_json: It portrays his sovereign care and authority over the churches and their angels
It means the churches are distant from him
It shows the stars rule over Jesus
It identifies John as the keeper of the churches
correct_answer: It portrays his sovereign care and authority over the churches and their angels
explanation: The stars represent the angels of the churches, and Jesus holds them in his right hand (Revelation 1:16, 20).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, why does the book begin with blessing for hearing and keeping rather than merely analyzing?
choices_json: Apocalyptic revelation is meant to form faithful obedience, not just curiosity
The visions are too vague to understand
Only John is expected to obey the message
The churches are praised regardless of response
correct_answer: Apocalyptic revelation is meant to form faithful obedience, not just curiosity
explanation: The blessing is linked to reading, hearing, and keeping the prophecy (Revelation 1:3).

prompt: According to Revelation 1, what does the vision imply about the relationship between heaven's authority and the churches on earth?
choices_json: The churches on earth are directly addressed and governed by the exalted Christ from heaven
The churches are left to govern without divine involvement
Heaven speaks only through political rulers
John replaces Christ as heavenly mediator
correct_answer: The churches on earth are directly addressed and governed by the exalted Christ from heaven
explanation: The glorified Christ appears, interprets the vision, and directs messages to the churches (Revelation 1:10-20).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, which church is told that it has left its first love?
correct_answer: Ephesus
explanation: Jesus tells Ephesus that it left its first love (Revelation 2:4).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, which church is told that it will have tribulation for ten days?
correct_answer: Smyrna
explanation: Jesus tells Smyrna that it will have tribulation ten days (Revelation 2:10).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, where was Satan's throne said to be?
correct_answer: Pergamum
explanation: Jesus says Pergamum is where Satan's throne is (Revelation 2:13).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, who is the false prophetess mentioned in the message to Thyatira?
correct_answer: Jezebel
explanation: Jesus says Thyatira tolerates the woman Jezebel (Revelation 2:20).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what tree is promised to the one who overcomes in Ephesus?
correct_answer: The tree of life
explanation: The one who overcomes is promised to eat from the tree of life (Revelation 2:7).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what crown is promised to the faithful in Smyrna?
correct_answer: The crown of life
explanation: Jesus tells Smyrna to be faithful to death and he will give the crown of life (Revelation 2:10).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what is promised to the one who overcomes in Thyatira?
choices_json: Authority over the nations and the morning star
A white stone and hidden manna
correct_answer: Authority over the nations and the morning star
explanation: The overcomer in Thyatira receives authority over the nations and the morning star (Revelation 2:26-28).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what is praised about Ephesus before it is rebuked?
choices_json: Its works, toil, perseverance, and testing of false apostles
correct_answer: Its works, toil, perseverance, and testing of false apostles
explanation: Jesus praises Ephesus for these qualities before rebuking it for leaving its first love (Revelation 2:2-4).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what is Ephesus commanded to do after remembering from where it fell?
choices_json: Repent and do the first works
correct_answer: Repent and do the first works
explanation: Jesus tells Ephesus to remember, repent, and do the first works (Revelation 2:5).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what does Jesus say about the poverty of Smyrna?
choices_json: It is poor, but actually rich
It is poor because it lacks faith
It will become rich through compromise
It must leave the city to survive
correct_answer: It is poor, but actually rich
explanation: Jesus says, 'I know your poverty, but you are rich' (Revelation 2:9).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what should Smyrna not fear?
choices_json: The things it is about to suffer
correct_answer: The things it is about to suffer
explanation: Jesus tells Smyrna, 'Don't be afraid of the things which you are about to suffer' (Revelation 2:10).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, whose teaching was present in Pergamum?
choices_json: The teaching of Balaam and the teaching of the Nicolaitans
The teaching of Jezebel and Korah
The teaching of the Pharisees only
The teaching of the Sadducees only
correct_answer: The teaching of Balaam and the teaching of the Nicolaitans
explanation: Jesus says some in Pergamum hold the teaching of Balaam and likewise the teaching of the Nicolaitans (Revelation 2:14-15).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what is promised to the one who overcomes in Pergamum?
choices_json: Hidden manna and a white stone with a new name
The right to the tree of life
To be clothed in white garments
correct_answer: Hidden manna and a white stone with a new name
explanation: Jesus promises hidden manna and a white stone with a new name to the overcomer in Pergamum (Revelation 2:17).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what is praised about Thyatira?
choices_json: Its love, faith, service, perseverance, and increasing works
correct_answer: Its love, faith, service, perseverance, and increasing works
explanation: Jesus praises Thyatira for these things, saying its last works are more than the first (Revelation 2:19).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what had Jezebel been teaching and leading Christ's servants to do?
choices_json: Commit sexual immorality and eat things sacrificed to idols
correct_answer: Commit sexual immorality and eat things sacrificed to idols
explanation: Jezebel is said to seduce Christ's servants to commit sexual immorality and eat idol sacrifices (Revelation 2:20).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what is the main danger in Ephesus's condition?
choices_json: Orthodoxy and endurance had continued, but love had cooled
It had fully embraced idol worship
It had denied Christ under persecution
correct_answer: Orthodoxy and endurance had continued, but love had cooled
explanation: Ephesus is praised for discernment and perseverance, yet rebuked for leaving its first love (Revelation 2:2-4).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what does the message to Smyrna show about how Jesus views suffering churches?
choices_json: He knows their affliction and calls them rich even in poverty
He tells them to compromise to survive
He promises immediate escape from all trial
correct_answer: He knows their affliction and calls them rich even in poverty
explanation: Jesus knows Smyrna's tribulation and poverty and declares them rich (Revelation 2:9).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what does Antipas's mention in Pergamum highlight?
choices_json: Faithful witness is possible even where Satan's throne is
Pergamum had no faithful believers left
Only leaders are called to witness
correct_answer: Faithful witness is possible even where Satan's throne is
explanation: Antipas is called Jesus' faithful witness who was killed there, yet Christ commends the church for holding fast his name (Revelation 2:13).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what is the effect of tolerating Jezebel in Thyatira?
choices_json: A church with many strengths is endangered by tolerated corruption
The church becomes stronger through broader inclusion
Only outsiders are affected by false teaching
It shows that doctrine matters less than service
correct_answer: A church with many strengths is endangered by tolerated corruption
explanation: Thyatira is commended for much good, yet sharply rebuked for tolerating Jezebel's corrupting influence (Revelation 2:19-20).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what does the phrase 'the synagogue of Satan' in the Smyrna message emphasize?
choices_json: Some who claim covenant privilege are acting in opposition to God's people
Every synagogue is condemned without distinction
The church should reject the Old Testament
correct_answer: Some who claim covenant privilege are acting in opposition to God's people
explanation: Jesus refers to those who say they are Jews and are not, but are a synagogue of Satan (Revelation 2:9).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what do the promises to overcomers suggest about perseverance?
choices_json: Faithfulness under pressure leads to life, fellowship, and vindication from Christ
Overcoming is only for a special elite class
Perseverance earns salvation apart from grace
The promises apply only to martyrs
correct_answer: Faithfulness under pressure leads to life, fellowship, and vindication from Christ
explanation: The promises include the tree of life, freedom from the second death, hidden manna, a new name, authority, and the morning star (Revelation 2:7, 11, 17, 26-28).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, why is repentance demanded from churches as well as individuals?
choices_json: Corporate tolerance of evil brings corporate accountability before Christ
Only individuals are ever responsible for sin
Repentance is symbolic and not urgent
Churches cannot really change after falling
correct_answer: Corporate tolerance of evil brings corporate accountability before Christ
explanation: Ephesus, Pergamum, and Thyatira are addressed as churches and warned to repent of their condition or tolerance of evil (Revelation 2:5, 16, 22).

prompt: How do the four messages in Revelation 2 together portray Jesus' knowledge of the churches?
choices_json: He sees both hidden faithfulness and hidden compromise with exact clarity
He judges only public actions and not motives
He speaks the same word to every church without distinction
He leaves correction to local leaders alone
correct_answer: He sees both hidden faithfulness and hidden compromise with exact clarity
explanation: Each message begins from Christ's knowledge and addresses the precise condition of each church (Revelation 2:2, 9, 13, 19).

prompt: How do Ephesus and Thyatira together warn against incomplete faithfulness?
choices_json: One church had truth without love, while the other had love and works but tolerated falsehood
Both churches were condemned only for poverty
Both churches had denied Christ openly
correct_answer: One church had truth without love, while the other had love and works but tolerated falsehood
explanation: Ephesus left its first love, while Thyatira had love and service yet tolerated Jezebel (Revelation 2:4, 20).

prompt: How does Revelation 2 connect present faithfulness with future reward?
choices_json: Each church's response now is linked to an overcoming promise later
Only one church receives any promise
Judgment is the only future theme
correct_answer: Each church's response now is linked to an overcoming promise later
explanation: Each message ends with a promise to the one who overcomes, connecting present perseverance with future blessing (Revelation 2:7, 11, 17, 26-28).

prompt: What role does persecution play across the messages in Revelation 2?
choices_json: Persecution tests some churches, while compromise endangers others from within
Every church is threatened in exactly the same way
External suffering automatically purifies all churches
correct_answer: Persecution tests some churches, while compromise endangers others from within
explanation: Smyrna and Pergamum face hostile pressure, while Ephesus, Pergamum, and Thyatira also face dangers of inner failure or compromise (Revelation 2:9-10, 13-16, 20).

prompt: Why is the warning about lampstand removal in Ephesus especially serious?
choices_json: A church can lose its place as Christ's recognized witness if it refuses to repent
It means salvation is impossible for any individual there
It refers only to a change of building location
It promises Ephesus greater public influence
correct_answer: A church can lose its place as Christ's recognized witness if it refuses to repent
explanation: Jesus warns he will remove Ephesus's lampstand from its place unless it repents (Revelation 2:5).

prompt: Why do the titles of Christ at the start of each message in Revelation 2 matter?
choices_json: They fit the specific needs and warnings of each church
They are decorative and unrelated to the message
They replace the actual content of the letters
They are hidden codes for political alliances
correct_answer: They fit the specific needs and warnings of each church
explanation: Christ is described differently to each church in ways that match the message, such as the one who was dead and came to life for Smyrna, and the one with the sharp two-edged sword for Pergamum (Revelation 2:1, 8, 12, 18).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what does the repeated call 'He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches' imply?
choices_json: Each message is specific to one church but relevant to all the churches
Only prophets can understand the letters
The Spirit speaks apart from Christ's words
Hearing is less important than outward ritual
correct_answer: Each message is specific to one church but relevant to all the churches
explanation: Though each letter is addressed to one church, the call is to hear what the Spirit says to the churches collectively (Revelation 2:7, 11, 17, 29).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, how does the chapter hold together holiness and encouragement?
choices_json: Christ sharply confronts sin while still promising rich reward to overcomers
Christ judges but never offers hope
Holiness is reserved for one church only
correct_answer: Christ sharply confronts sin while still promising rich reward to overcomers
explanation: The messages include rebuke and warning, yet each also holds out promises to those who overcome (Revelation 2:5, 10-11, 16-17, 24-28).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, why is compromise with idolatry and immorality presented as so dangerous?
choices_json: It directly opposes loyalty to Christ and corrupts the church's witness
It is permitted when persecution grows severe
It is a minor matter compared with church growth
correct_answer: It directly opposes loyalty to Christ and corrupts the church's witness
explanation: The teachings of Balaam and Jezebel lead Christ's servants into idolatry and immorality, bringing Christ's judgment unless there is repentance (Revelation 2:14, 20-23).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what theological contrast appears between Smyrna and the compromising churches?
choices_json: Smyrna suffers outwardly but is spiritually rich, while others appear active yet harbor compromise
Smyrna prospers outwardly but is spiritually poor
All four churches are condemned equally
Only persecuted churches matter to Christ
correct_answer: Smyrna suffers outwardly but is spiritually rich, while others appear active yet harbor compromise
explanation: Smyrna is poor yet rich, while other churches face rebuke for lost love or tolerated falsehood despite strengths (Revelation 2:4, 9, 14-15, 20).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what does the promise of a 'new name' in Pergamum most naturally suggest?
choices_json: Personal acceptance and identity given by Christ to the faithful
A political title recognized by Rome
A secret password for escaping martyrdom
The replacement of repentance with privilege
correct_answer: Personal acceptance and identity given by Christ to the faithful
explanation: The white stone and new name known by the receiver point to intimate recognition and belonging from Christ (Revelation 2:17).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, why does Jesus describe himself in Thyatira as the one whose eyes are like a flame of fire and feet like burnished brass?
choices_json: He searches deeply and judges firmly in a church tolerating corruption
He mainly wants to inspire artistic imagery
He is identifying himself as an angel
He is promising military conquest for Thyatira
correct_answer: He searches deeply and judges firmly in a church tolerating corruption
explanation: In the Thyatira message Jesus later says that all the churches will know he searches minds and hearts and repays works (Revelation 2:18, 23).

prompt: According to Revelation 2, what does the chapter teach about faithful endurance?
choices_json: It is not passive survival but loving, truthful, repentant loyalty to Christ under pressure
It means avoiding all conflict by compromise
It requires outward success in every church
It belongs only to those who never suffer
correct_answer: It is not passive survival but loving, truthful, repentant loyalty to Christ under pressure
explanation: Across the four messages, endurance includes first works, faithfulness unto death, rejection of false teaching, and holding fast what Christ gave (Revelation 2:5, 10, 16, 25).

prompt: According to Revelation 3, which church is said to have a name that it lives, but is dead?
correct_answer: Sardis
explanation: Jesus tells Sardis, 'You have a name that you live, and are dead' (Revelation 3:1).

prompt: According to Revelation 3, which church is told, 'I have set before you an open door'?
correct_answer: Philadelphia
explanation: Jesus tells Philadelphia that he has set before it an open door (Revelation 3:8).

prompt: According to Revelation 3, which church is described as lukewarm?
correct_answer: Laodicea
explanation: Jesus says Laodicea is neither cold nor hot, but lukewarm (Revelation 3:15-16).

prompt: According to Revelation 3, what color garments are promised to the overcomer in Sardis?
correct_answer: White garments
explanation: The one who overcomes in Sardis will be clothed in white garments (Revelation 3:5).

prompt: According to Revelation 3, what is promised to the overcomer in Philadelphia?
choices_json: To be made a pillar in the temple of God
To rule the nations with a rod of iron
To eat from the tree of life
correct_answer: To be made a pillar in the temple of God
explanation: Jesus promises to make the overcomer in Philadelphia a pillar in the temple of God (Revelation 3:12).

prompt: According to Revelation 3, what does Jesus stand at and knock?
correct_answer: The door
explanation: Jesus says, 'Behold, I stand at the door and knock' (Revelation 3:20).

prompt: According to Revelation 3, what seat is promised to the overcomer in Laodicea?
choices_json: To sit with Jesus on his throne
To sit among the twenty-four elders
To sit at the right hand of John
To sit in the temple court
correct_answer: To sit with Jesus on his throne
explanation: Jesus promises the overcomer to sit with him on his throne (Revelation 3:21).

prompt: According to Revelation 3, what is Sardis told to do first in response to its condition?
choices_json: Wake up and strengthen the things that remain
correct_answer: Wake up and strengthen the things that remain
explanation: Jesus tells Sardis to wake up and strengthen what remains, which was about to die (Revelation 3:2).

prompt: According to Revelation 3, how will Jesus come to Sardis if it does not watch?
correct_answer: Like a thief
explanation: If Sardis does not watch, Jesus says he will come as a thief (Revelation 3:3).

prompt: According to Revelation 3, what is praised about Philadelphia?
choices_json: It has a little power, kept Jesus' word, and did not deny his name
It is wealthy and needs nothing
It has tested false apostles perfectly
It has growing works but tolerates Jezebel
